summary: O'Neil dismantles the common defense that problematic algorithmic scores like teacher VAMs are acceptable because they're 'one of many' factors in decisions. She explains that when other evaluation components have little variance (most teachers rated 'acceptable' or 'excellent'), the VAM score was specifically introduced to create spread between teachers. This means even if VAM counts for only 50% of a score, it dominates the variance and therefore determines rankings. The mathematical reality is that mixing a noisy, unreliable measure with low-variance measures doesn't dilute its effect—it amplifies its importance in determining outcomes.
key_points: The 'one of many' defense is self-defeating: VAM was meant to have real, valuable effect, so arguing it has 'very little power' undermines its purpose
Most non-VAM teacher evaluation components (principal observations, peer reviews) have extremely low variance—nearly all teachers score similarly
VAM was introduced precisely to create variance and spread between teachers, enabling ranking
When you mix a high-variance measure with low-variance ones, the high-variance component dominates relative rankings regardless of weighting
The same logic applies to recidivism risk scores: if the most statistically variant input is flawed and racist, its effect on outcomes is amplified, not diluted
money_quotes: The use of something that is unfair is less unfair, in other words, if you also use other things which balance it out and are fair.
The very short version is that it's little better than a random number generator.
Any argument about it which basically implies that it's okay to use it because it has very little power seems odd and self-defeating.
The VAM was brought in precisely to introduce variance to the overall mix.
But if those VAM scores are actually meaningless, or at least extremely noisy, then what you have is 'spread' without accuracy.
The VAM score will comprise much more than 50% of the information that goes into the score.
Whoever was unlucky enough to get a bad VAM score will still be on the bottom.
The overall effect of using flawed recidivism risk scores is stronger, rather than weaker, than one might expect given its weighting.
key_insight: When a flawed algorithmic score is the only component with meaningful variance, it dominates outcomes regardless of its official weighting—making the 'one of many factors' defense mathematically hollow.
